source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - Ship Shoal 266-A Platform Fire

actions_taken: Life rafts and life floats deployed
Firefighting attempts with Purple K units and water reels
Offshore Supply Vessels responded and fought fire with water cannons
Isolation valve at bottom of interface sight glass closed post-incident
event.summary: On January 5, 2017, at approximately 2:00 a.m., a fire occurred on Ship Shoal 266-A platform when oil discharged from a failed interface sight glass on the Heater Treater (NBK-1700) saturated protective insulation and contacted the hot exhaust manifold of Pipeline Pump #1 (PAX-4100), located 14 feet away. The sight glass failure was exacerbated by missing ball checks in the gauge cock valves, which allowed continuous oil discharge. All four crew members were evacuated and rescued. The fire was extinguished at approximately 5:50 a.m. Total property damage was estimated at $2.7 million.
recommendations: BSEE should issue a Safety Alert to industry identifying possible issues pertaining to Sight Glass Assemblies, Gauge Cock Valves, and Ball Check Assemblies
Operators should refer to API RP 14J for Design and Hazards Analysis for Offshore Production Facilities
key_phrases: gauge cock valves not fully functional

hazards.name: Pressurized oil in Heater Treater vessel

threats.name: Interface sight glass failure and oil discharge
Missing or non-functional ball checks in gauge cock valves
Oil mist/spray contact with hot exhaust system
Proximity of Heater Treater to Pipeline Pump exhaust (14 feet)

consequences.name: Property damage to Heater Treater, electrical systems, and fixtures
Environmental release of crude oil (less than 5 barrels)
Personnel exposure to fire and evacuation hazards

controls.name: Ball check assemblies in gauge cock valves (top and bottom of sight glass)
controls.barrier_role: Prevent or mitigate oil discharge from sight glass failure
controls.lod_basis: Primary mechanical barrier to isolate pressurized vessel contents

supporting_text: the gauge cock valves located on the top and bottom of the Heater Treater's interface sight glass were not fully functional because there were no ball checks in either valve
If the ball checks were in place, they could have prevented the flow and/or mitigated the flow of oil due to interface sight glass breakage

controls.name: Interface sight glass assembly integrity and maintenance
controls.barrier_role: Maintain structural integrity of sight glass to prevent rupture
controls.lod_basis: Primary containment barrier for vessel interface level observation

supporting_text: a mist/spray of oil coming from the interface sight glass area of the Heater Treater
the interface sight glass was identified as compromised, it was at that time that it had begun to discharge vessel product (oil) with a force of approximately 30-40 psi
Other possibilities leading up to the failure of the sight glass could be exposure to sunlight over the years or excessive vibration on the platform while the pipeline pumps were operating

controls.name: Daily oil/water level checks and interface sight glass accuracy verification
controls.barrier_role: Detect sight glass degradation or anomalies through routine inspection

supporting_text: The oil/water level is checked for accuracy daily
the Night Operator stated that he had worked on the level of the interface sight glass at least twice that night--at some point between 6:00 p.m. and 7:00 p.m. and again at 10:00 p.m.

controls.name: Isolation valve at bottom of interface sight glass
controls.barrier_role: Stop oil discharge after sight glass failure is detected
controls.lod_basis: Secondary containment isolation following primary barrier failure

supporting_text: Operators closed the isolation valve located at the bottom of the interface sight glass
Due to the ball checks not being in place, a constant discharge of oil continued until the level of oil in the Heater Treater dropped below the level of the bottom gauge cock valve

controls.name: Spatial separation between Heater Treater and Pipeline Pump exhaust
controls.barrier_role: Prevent oil mist contact with ignition source
controls.lod_basis: Inherent design separation to prevent hazard interaction

supporting_text: The Heater Treater and Pipeline Pump #1 exhaust system are located only 14 feet apart
The close proximity of the Heater Treater to the Pipeline Pump allowed the fire to ignite

controls.name: Protective insulation on Heater Treater exterior
controls.barrier_role: Contain or slow oil discharge and reduce spray dispersion
controls.lod_basis: Secondary containment to limit oil mist spread

supporting_text: Oil from the interface sight glass saturated the protective insulation used to cover the exterior of the Heater Treater before discharging out towards the exhaust system of Pipeline Pump #1
As product (oil) discharged from the interface sight glass it saturated the protective insulation, external to the Heater Treater, and subsequently blew over to Pipeline Pump #1 exhaust system

controls.name: Emergency Shut Down (ESD) system activation
controls.barrier_role: Stop platform operations and reduce fuel supply to fire
controls.lod_basis: Automated emergency response to halt hazardous operations

supporting_text: He therefore activated the Emergency Shut Down (ESD) system near the living quarters and woke the rest of the crew
The fire kept reigniting on the side of the Heater Treater even after shutting in the platform

controls.name: Firefighting equipment (Purple K units and water reels)
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response equipment for fire suppression

supporting_text: All four crew members attempted to fight the fire utilizing two "Purple K" wheeled units, one portable "Purple K" unit, and four fixed water system reels, all unsuccessfully
firefighting efforts were compared to "putting water on a hot grease fire"

controls.name: Personnel evacuation and life-saving equipment deployment
controls.barrier_role: Protect personnel from fire hazard through evacuation
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response to prevent personnel injury or fatality

supporting_text: the Lead Operator, acting as the platform's Person In Charge (PIC), utilized Ultimate Work Authority (UWA) and instructed all personnel to evacuate the structure
the personnel onboard the platform launched one 16-man life raft and two 12-man life floats
OSV Mary Wyatt Milano retrieved two of the crew members from the water, as well as the remaining two crew members from the boat landing

controls.name: Operator rounds and visual inspection of equipment
controls.barrier_role: Detect sight glass failure or oil discharge early
controls.lod_basis: Routine operational monitoring and hazard detection

supporting_text: the A-Operator working the night shift was making rounds on the main deck near Pipeline Pump #1 (PAX-4100) located in the generator area, when he noticed a mist/spray of oil coming from the interface sight glass area
At about 2:00 a.m., the Night Operator noticed a mist/spray coming from the interface sight glass area at the Heater Treater while conducting water skimming operations

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
